contradictions.claim_text: EU-wide coverage applies to all companies.
contradictions.resolution: Rewrite the scope message. Article 4 applies to providers, deployers, and other in-scope actors, and it can also reach some actors outside the EU when their systems are used in the Union or affect people in the Union.
contradictions.why_it_matters: Leaving the legacy wording in place would materially overstate the addressable compliance market.

open_issues.claim_text: Most AI literacy training is English-only.
open_issues.notes: The rerun surfaced a French paid alternative and a multilingual EU public alternative, so the blanket language-gap claim cannot be treated as a fact.

open_issues.claim_text: EU-wide remote delivery is enough to solve the buyer's AI literacy problem.
open_issues.notes: No reviewed source proves that one remote format is sufficient across country, sector, and use-context differences.

open_issues.claim_text: The multilingual FR/DE/EN angle is a durable geographic advantage.
open_issues.notes: Elements of AI already offers English, French, and German among many other languages, and House of Training provides a French paid alternative.

falsification_searches.result: The Commission Q&A narrows the obligation to providers, deployers, and other in-scope actors rather than every EU company.

falsification_searches.query: "Elements of AI" multilingual English French German free self-paced
falsification_searches.result: The EU Digital Skills and Jobs Platform and University of Helsinki sources show a free multilingual self-paced substitute with EU country coverage.
House of Training publicly lists a paid 4h French face-to-face AI fundamentals course, which weakens any blanket language-gap story.
